title: Financial Promotions Regime for Cryptoassets — Overview
content: Section 21 of the Financial Services and Markets Act 2000 (FSMA) establishes the financial promotion restriction: a person must not, in the course of business, communicate an invitation or inducement to engage in investment activity unless the person is an authorised person or the content of the communication is approved by an authorised person. Effective 8 October 2023, the UK government extended this regime to qualifying cryptoassets through the Financial Services and Markets Act 2000 (Financial Promotion) (Amendment) Order 2023 (SI 2023/612). Qualifying cryptoassets are defined as any cryptographically secured digital representation of value or contractual rights that is fungible and transferable, excluding cryptoassets that are already regulated financial instruments (e-money, security tokens) and non-fungible tokens (NFTs). The extension means that all promotions of qualifying cryptoassets to UK consumers — including those by overseas firms — must comply with FCA rules or fall within an exemption. The FCA has stated that firms marketing cryptoassets to UK consumers must either be FCA-authorised, have their promotions approved by an FCA-authorised person, be registered with the FCA under the Money Laundering Regulations for cryptoasset activities, or communicate promotions under an applicable exemption. Non-compliance is a criminal offence under Section 25 FSMA carrying up to two years imprisonment.

article: FCA PS23/6 — Final Rules on Cryptoasset Promotions
title: PS23/6 — Cryptoasset Financial Promotions Final Rules
content: In June 2023, the FCA published Policy Statement PS23/6, setting out final rules for the promotion of cryptoassets effective 8 October 2023. The rules apply to all firms marketing qualifying cryptoassets to UK consumers, including overseas firms. Key requirements include: all cryptoasset promotions must include clear risk warnings, specifically the prescribed warning that cryptoassets are high-risk and largely unregulated, and that consumers should be prepared to lose all their money. The FCA's prescribed risk warning text states 'Don't invest unless you're prepared to lose all the money you invest. This is a high-risk investment and you should not expect to be protected if something goes wrong.' Promotions must be clear, fair, and not misleading under COBS 4. The rules introduce a mandatory 24-hour cooling-off period for first-time investors, during which the firm must not complete the transaction. Refer-a-friend bonuses and similar incentives are banned. Firms must conduct a client categorisation assessment and an appropriateness assessment to determine whether the consumer has sufficient knowledge and experience to understand the risks of investing in cryptoassets. Direct offer financial promotions require enhanced risk acknowledgements from consumers. These rules closely mirror the FCA's approach to other high-risk investments such as speculative illiquid securities and peer-to-peer lending agreements.

title: FCA Registration Under MLR 2017 for Cryptoasset Businesses
content: Under Part 8A of the Money Laundering, Terrorist Financing and Transfer of Funds (Information on the Payer) Regulations 2017 (MLR 2017), as amended by the Money Laundering and Terrorist Financing (Amendment) Regulations 2019 (SI 2019/1511), cryptoasset exchange providers and custodian wallet providers must register with the FCA before commencing business in the UK. A cryptoasset exchange provider is defined as a firm that exchanges cryptoassets for money or money for cryptoassets, or exchanges one cryptoasset for another. A custodian wallet provider is a firm that provides services to safeguard or administer cryptoassets or private cryptographic keys on behalf of customers. Registration requires the firm to demonstrate compliance with the MLR 2017 including customer due diligence, ongoing monitoring, suspicious activity reporting, record-keeping, and governance requirements. The FCA conducts a rigorous assessment of applications including evaluation of the firm's AML/CFT framework, the fitness and propriety of key individuals, the firm's business model, and its financial crime risk assessment. The FCA has rejected a significant proportion of applications — as of 2023, only approximately 42 firms had been registered, while over 200 applications were refused, withdrawn, or rejected. Registration under the MLR does not constitute FCA authorisation and does not bring the firm within the scope of the Financial Services Compensation Scheme (FSCS) or the Financial Ombudsman Service (FOS).

title: Specified Investment Test for Security Tokens
content: The FCA's Perimeter Guidance Manual (PERG), particularly PERG 2.6, sets out the framework for determining whether a cryptoasset constitutes a specified investment (security) under the Financial Services and Markets Act 2000 (Regulated Activities) Order 2001 (RAO). The FCA classifies cryptoassets into three broad categories: security tokens, which provide rights and obligations akin to specified investments such as shares (RAO Article 76), debt instruments (RAO Article 77), units in collective investment schemes (RAO Article 81), and warrants (RAO Article 79); e-money tokens, which are tokens that meet the definition of electronic money under the Electronic Money Regulations 2011; and unregulated tokens, which include utility tokens and exchange tokens (such as Bitcoin and Ether) that do not meet the definition of specified investments or e-money. Security tokens are fully within the FCA's regulatory perimeter and firms dealing in, arranging deals in, advising on, or managing security tokens must be FCA-authorised under the appropriate permissions. The FCA published PS19/22 Guidance on Cryptoassets in July 2019 providing detailed guidance on classification. The determination is fact-specific and turns on the rights and obligations attached to the token, not its label. A token marketed as a utility token but conferring profit-sharing rights or ownership interests may be classified as a security token.

title: UK Travel Rule for Cryptoasset Transfers
content: The UK implemented its crypto-specific Travel Rule through the Money Laundering and Terrorist Financing (Amendment) (No. 2) Regulations 2022, effective 1 September 2023. Under the UK Travel Rule, cryptoasset businesses must collect, verify, and transmit originator and beneficiary information for cryptoasset transfers. For transfers of GBP 1,000 or more, the originating firm must obtain and transmit to the beneficiary firm the originator's full name, the originator's account number or unique transaction identifier, and either the originator's address, date of birth, place of birth, or customer identification number. The beneficiary firm's name and account number or unique identifier must also be transmitted. For transfers below GBP 1,000, firms must still collect the originator's and beneficiary's names and account numbers, but verification is only required where there is suspicion of money laundering or terrorist financing. For transfers to or from unhosted wallets (self-custodial wallets), the firm must collect the same information but does not need to transmit it (as there is no receiving firm). However, the firm must conduct a risk assessment of the transfer and may need to apply enhanced due diligence. The Joint Money Laundering Steering Group (JMLSG) has published sector-specific guidance for cryptoasset firms on Travel Rule compliance. Non-compliance with the Travel Rule can result in civil penalties and criminal prosecution under the MLR 2017.

title: Consumer Duty Obligations for Crypto Firms
content: The FCA's Consumer Duty, introduced through Policy Statement PS22/9 and effective 31 July 2023, establishes a new consumer protection standard applicable to all FCA-authorised and registered firms, including cryptoasset businesses registered under the MLR 2017. The Consumer Duty comprises a Consumer Principle requiring firms to act to deliver good outcomes for retail customers, supported by three cross-cutting rules: act in good faith toward retail customers, avoid causing foreseeable harm, and enable and support customers to pursue their financial objectives. The Duty is underpinned by four outcomes: products and services must be designed to meet the needs of the target market and provide fair value (Products and Services outcome); the price of products must represent fair value for customers (Price and Value outcome); consumers must be equipped with the information they need to make effective decisions (Consumer Understanding outcome); and firms must provide a level of support that meets the needs of customers (Consumer Support outcome). For cryptoasset firms, the Consumer Duty requires clear and prominent disclosure of risks, fair pricing transparency, accessible customer support, and products designed with the target retail customer in mind. The FCA has stated that it expects firms to consider the Consumer Duty in the context of the inherent risks of cryptoasset investments and the generally lower levels of financial literacy among some crypto investors.

title: FCA Action Against Binance Markets Limited
content: In June 2021, the FCA issued a supervisory notice requiring Binance Markets Limited (BML) — the UK entity of the Binance group — to cease carrying out any regulated activities in the UK without the prior written consent of the FCA. The FCA also published a consumer warning that Binance group entities were not authorised or registered to conduct any regulated activity in the UK. The action was taken on the basis that BML was not capable of being effectively supervised by the FCA, with the FCA identifying concerns about BML's compliance with AML requirements, the complexity and global scope of the Binance group's operations, and the adequacy of BML's governance and systems and controls. Separately, the FCA published notices stating that no entity in the Binance Group held any form of UK authorisation, registration, or licence to conduct regulated activity in the UK. The enforcement action effectively barred Binance from operating regulated crypto services for UK customers and was one of the most high-profile regulatory actions against a global crypto exchange. The action highlighted the FCA's willingness to take decisive supervisory action against firms it considers unable to meet UK regulatory standards, even where the firm's parent group is a major global crypto exchange.

title: FCA Supervisory Actions Against Crypto Exchanges
content: The FCA has taken supervisory action against multiple crypto firms operating in the UK market. In relation to Payward Ltd (Kraken's UK entity), the FCA placed requirements on the firm's operations in the UK, including restrictions on the services it could offer to UK retail customers. The FCA identified concerns about the firm's financial promotions compliance, particularly after the October 2023 financial promotions regime came into effect, and about the adequacy of its systems for ensuring compliance with the UK's AML/CFT requirements. The FCA has also taken action against numerous smaller crypto firms. As of March 2024, the FCA had issued over 1,700 consumer alerts and warnings regarding unauthorised crypto firms targeting UK consumers, including firms fraudulently claiming FCA registration. The FCA operates a Warning List that includes both firms that are definitively scams and firms that may be operating without proper authorisation. The FCA has also used its powers under the MLR 2017 to refuse registration applications where firms failed to demonstrate adequate AML controls. The high volume of consumer alerts reflects the FCA's assessment that financial crime risk remains the most significant risk in the UK cryptoasset sector, with many consumers being targeted by fraud involving cryptoassets.

title: AML/CFT Obligations for Registered Cryptoasset Firms
content: Cryptoasset firms registered with the FCA under the MLR 2017 must comply with comprehensive AML/CFT obligations. These include conducting a business-wide risk assessment identifying and assessing the money laundering and terrorist financing risks to which the firm is subject, having regard to the risk factors including customer risk, geographic risk, product and service risk, transaction risk, and delivery channel risk. Firms must implement policies, controls, and procedures to effectively manage and mitigate the identified risks. Customer due diligence (CDD) must be conducted when establishing a business relationship, carrying out an occasional transaction of EUR 15,000 or more (or its equivalent), when there is suspicion of money laundering or terrorist financing, or when there are doubts about previously obtained identification data. CDD measures include identifying the customer and verifying their identity using reliable and independent sources, identifying the beneficial owner and taking reasonable measures to verify their identity, and assessing and obtaining information on the purpose and intended nature of the business relationship. Enhanced due diligence is required for higher-risk situations including politically exposed persons (PEPs). Firms must conduct ongoing monitoring of business relationships and must file suspicious activity reports (SARs) with the National Crime Agency (NCA). Records must be retained for at least five years.

article: Financial Services and Markets Act 2023, Sections 69-72
title: Future Regulatory Regime Under FSMA 2023 Amendments
content: The Financial Services and Markets Act 2023 (FSMA 2023), which received Royal Assent on 29 June 2023, provides the legislative foundation for a comprehensive UK regulatory framework for cryptoassets. Sections 69 through 72 of FSMA 2023 amend the original FSMA 2000 to bring cryptoassets within the scope of the UK's financial services regulatory framework. Section 69 grants HM Treasury the power to designate activities relating to cryptoassets as regulated activities under the RAO, meaning that firms carrying out such activities would require FCA authorisation rather than merely MLR registration. Section 71 provides for the regulation of cryptoasset issuance and disclosures, establishing a framework for admissions and disclosures analogous to the prospectus regime for securities. Section 72 grants the FCA powers over cryptoasset market abuse, including insider dealing and market manipulation in relation to qualifying cryptoassets. HM Treasury published a consultation and call for evidence in February 2023 on the future regulatory framework, proposing a phased approach beginning with fiat-backed stablecoins, followed by broader cryptoasset activities. The transition from MLR registration to full FCA authorisation will represent a significant step-up in regulatory requirements for UK crypto firms.

title: UK Stablecoin Regulatory Framework
content: The Financial Services and Markets Act 2023 provides for the regulation of fiat-backed stablecoins used as a means of payment within the UK. HM Treasury has identified fiat-backed stablecoins as the first category of cryptoassets to be brought within the full regulatory perimeter. Under the proposed framework, issuers of fiat-backed stablecoins used for payment in the UK would need to be authorised by the FCA and would be subject to requirements including maintaining reserves of backing assets at least equal to the value of tokens in circulation at all times, with reserves held in high-quality liquid assets; providing holders with a legally enforceable right to redeem their tokens at par value on demand; meeting organisational and governance requirements comparable to those for e-money issuers; complying with prudential requirements including own funds requirements; and publishing regular attestation reports on the composition and adequacy of reserve assets. The Bank of England would have systemic oversight of stablecoins that reach systemic significance under the Financial Market Infrastructure special administration regime. The Payment Systems Regulator (PSR) would regulate stablecoin payment systems. This represents a different approach from the EU's MiCA, which regulates all stablecoins from inception rather than focusing initially on payment use cases.

article: FCA CP22/2 — Strengthening Financial Promotion Rules for High-Risk Investments
title: Risk Warning and Appropriateness Assessment Requirements
content: FCA Consultation Paper CP22/2 and subsequent Policy Statement PS22/10 established strengthened financial promotion rules for high-risk investments including cryptoassets, effective from February 2023 (with crypto-specific rules from October 2023). The rules require firms promoting cryptoassets to classify them as Restricted Mass Market Investments, meaning they can be promoted to retail consumers but subject to significant restrictions. Firms must ensure that marketing materials include a specific prescribed risk warning prominently displayed and not obscured by other content. Before a retail consumer can respond to a direct offer financial promotion for cryptoassets, the firm must conduct an appropriateness assessment to evaluate whether the consumer has sufficient knowledge and experience to understand the risks. The assessment must include questions about the consumer's understanding of what cryptoassets are, the risks of investing (including the risk of losing all money invested), and whether the investment is covered by the FSCS or FOS (it is not). If the assessment indicates the investment is not appropriate, the firm must provide a personalised risk warning. The firm must also apply a 24-hour cooling-off period for first-time investors. Incentives to invest, including refer-a-friend bonuses, free crypto offers, and new joiner bonuses, are prohibited. These rules apply to all firms promoting to UK consumers regardless of where the firm is based.

title: FCA Powers to Cancel or Suspend MLR Registration
content: Under Regulation 74A of the MLR 2017, the FCA has the power to cancel or suspend the registration of a cryptoasset firm where the firm has failed to comply with the requirements of the Regulations, has not carried on business for a period of at least 12 months, has failed to pay the required fees, or where any information provided to the FCA in connection with the application was false or misleading. The FCA may also cancel registration where the firm's officers are not fit and proper to carry out their functions, or where the firm's AML/CFT policies and procedures are inadequate. Before cancelling a registration, the FCA must give the firm a warning notice and a decision notice, and the firm has the right to refer the matter to the Upper Tribunal. However, in cases of urgency, the FCA may exercise its power to cancel registration immediately. The FCA has used these powers to cancel the registration of multiple cryptoasset firms and has published notices of cancellation on its website. The FCA also maintains the power to impose civil penalties for breaches of the MLR 2017, and can pursue criminal prosecution for serious offences including failure to register, tipping off, and failures to comply with the regulations. The FCA's approach has been characterised as robust, with a stated willingness to use the full range of enforcement tools available.

title: Suspicious Activity Reporting Obligations for Crypto Firms
content: Under Section 330 of the Proceeds of Crime Act 2002 (POCA) and Section 21A of the Terrorism Act 2000, persons in the regulated sector — including cryptoasset firms registered with the FCA — have a legal obligation to report suspicious activity to the National Crime Agency (NCA). A regulated sector person commits an offence if they know or suspect, or have reasonable grounds for knowing or suspecting, that another person is engaged in money laundering, and they do not make the required disclosure to the NCA as soon as practicable. The disclosure is made through a Suspicious Activity Report (SAR) submitted to the UK Financial Intelligence Unit (UKFIU) at the NCA. Failure to file a SAR when required is a criminal offence carrying a maximum penalty of five years imprisonment. Tipping off — informing a customer or any other person that a SAR has been filed or that a money laundering investigation is being conducted — is also a criminal offence under Section 333A POCA. Cryptoasset firms must implement systems and training to identify red flags for suspicious activity, including transactions involving known ransomware addresses, darknet marketplaces, mixing services, high-risk jurisdictions, and unusual patterns of behaviour. The NCA publishes annual SAR statistics and has noted a significant increase in SARs filed by the crypto sector since the introduction of MLR registration.

title: E-Money Token Classification Under UK Law
content: Under UK law, certain cryptoassets that represent electronically stored monetary value on receipt of funds for the purpose of making payment transactions may qualify as electronic money (e-money) under the Electronic Money Regulations 2011 (EMR 2011). E-money issuers must be authorised or registered by the FCA as electronic money institutions or be authorised as credit institutions. E-money tokens that qualify under the EMR 2011 are subject to requirements including safeguarding of customer funds equivalent to the amount of e-money in circulation, redeemability at par value on demand, capital requirements including initial capital of at least GBP 350,000 for authorised institutions, and ongoing own funds requirements calculated as 2% of the average outstanding e-money. E-money institutions must comply with the FCA's conduct-of-business requirements and the Payment Services Regulations 2017 where applicable. The distinction between e-money tokens and exchange tokens (like Bitcoin) or utility tokens is critical: e-money tokens are already within the FCA's regulatory perimeter under existing legislation, while exchange tokens were brought within scope only through the MLR 2017 amendments for AML purposes and the 2023 financial promotions extension. Stablecoins pegged to sterling or other currencies may qualify as e-money depending on their specific characteristics.

title: FCA Final Guidance on Cryptoasset Classification
content: In July 2019, the FCA published Policy Statement PS19/22 providing final guidance on the classification of cryptoassets. The guidance establishes a taxonomy with three principal categories. Exchange tokens (such as Bitcoin and Ether) are decentralised tokens used primarily as a means of exchange, are not issued or backed by any central authority, and do not provide the holder with any rights analogous to specified investments — these are outside the FCA's regulatory perimeter except for AML purposes. Utility tokens provide holders with access to a current or prospective product or service but do not grant rights equivalent to specified investments — these are generally outside the perimeter unless they also exhibit characteristics of specified investments. Security tokens provide holders with rights comparable to specified investments such as shares, debentures, units in a collective investment scheme, or rights to participate in a profit-sharing arrangement — these are fully within the FCA's regulatory perimeter and firms dealing in them require FCA authorisation. The FCA emphasised that classification depends on the substance and rights attached to the token, not its label. A token may change classification over its lifecycle — for example, a token that is initially offered as a security token may transition to a utility token once the underlying platform is operational and the token is used exclusively for its intended consumptive purpose.

title: Appropriateness Assessment for Cryptoasset Investors
content: Under the FCA's Conduct of Business Sourcebook (COBS), chapter 10, firms must assess the appropriateness of a non-advised service for a retail client before allowing them to proceed. For cryptoasset promotions, the FCA has mandated specific appropriateness assessment requirements effective October 2023. The assessment must evaluate whether the client has the necessary experience and knowledge to understand the risks involved in trading cryptoassets. The assessment must include questions addressing the client's understanding of the nature of cryptoassets and how they differ from regulated investments, the risks specific to cryptoassets including total loss of capital, market volatility, liquidity risk, and technology risk, the fact that cryptoasset investments are not covered by the Financial Services Compensation Scheme (FSCS), and the fact that the Financial Ombudsman Service (FOS) generally does not handle complaints about unregulated cryptoasset activities. If the client fails the appropriateness assessment, the firm must issue a personalised risk warning and may allow the client to proceed only after acknowledging the warning. The firm must re-assess appropriateness at least every 24 months. Firms must maintain records of appropriateness assessments and their outcomes. These requirements are significantly more prescriptive than the general COBS 10 appropriateness assessment, reflecting the FCA's heightened concern about consumer harm in the cryptoasset sector.

article: UK Sanctions and Anti-Money Laundering Act 2018
title: UK Sanctions Compliance for Cryptoasset Firms
content: Cryptoasset firms operating in the UK must comply with the UK's sanctions regime, administered by the Office of Financial Sanctions Implementation (OFSI) within HM Treasury. The Sanctions and Anti-Money Laundering Act 2018 (SAMLA) provides the legal framework for UK autonomous sanctions following Brexit. Under SAMLA and specific sanctions regulations (such as the Russia (Sanctions) (EU Exit) Regulations 2019 as amended), it is a criminal offence to deal with funds or economic resources owned, held, or controlled by a designated person, to make funds or economic resources available to or for the benefit of a designated person, or to circumvent financial sanctions. For cryptoassets specifically, OFSI has confirmed that cryptoassets constitute economic resources within the meaning of financial sanctions legislation, and that firms facilitating cryptoasset transactions must screen against the UK Sanctions List (the Consolidated List of Financial Sanctions Targets). In March 2022, following Russia's invasion of Ukraine, the UK introduced specific restrictions on crypto services to Russia, prohibiting the provision of crypto exchange and custody wallet services to persons connected with Russia where the value exceeds GBP 10,000. OFSI has the power to impose monetary penalties of up to GBP 1 million or 50% of the estimated value of the breach for sanctions violations on a strict liability basis.

article: FCA Dear CEO Letter — Crypto Risks (March 2023)
title: FCA Supervisory Priorities for Cryptoasset Firms
content: In March 2023, the FCA issued a Dear CEO letter to cryptoasset firms setting out its supervisory priorities and expectations. The FCA identified the following key risks: financial crime risk remains the most significant risk in the cryptoasset sector, with the UK seeing a substantial increase in crypto-related fraud; many registered firms had inadequate AML/CFT frameworks, particularly in the areas of transaction monitoring, sanctions screening, and suspicious activity reporting; governance and oversight at many firms was insufficient, with boards and senior management lacking adequate understanding of the specific risks associated with cryptoasset activities; consumer protection risks were heightened by misleading marketing and inadequate risk disclosures. The FCA set expectations that firms must review and strengthen their AML/CFT frameworks, ensure senior management engagement with financial crime risk, prepare for the October 2023 financial promotions regime, and ensure adequate technology and cybersecurity controls. The FCA warned that it would use the full range of its regulatory powers against firms that fail to meet these expectations, including cancellation of registration, publication of consumer warnings, and referral for criminal investigation. The letter reflected the FCA's increasingly assertive supervisory posture toward the cryptoasset sector and signalled its intention to raise standards ahead of the broader regulatory regime under FSMA 2023.

title: Insider Dealing and Market Abuse in Cryptoassets
content: Currently, the UK's criminal insider dealing provisions under Part V of the Criminal Justice Act 1993 and the civil market abuse regime under the Market Abuse Regulation (as retained in UK law post-Brexit) apply only to qualifying investments traded on regulated markets and prescribed markets. Most cryptoassets traded on unregulated crypto exchanges are therefore not currently covered by these provisions. However, the Financial Services and Markets Act 2023 contains powers for the government to extend the market abuse regime to cryptoassets. Section 72 of FSMA 2023 provides HM Treasury with the power to make regulations to apply prohibitions on insider dealing and market manipulation to qualifying cryptoassets, regardless of whether they are traded on a regulated market. The FCA has stated that once these powers are exercised, it will be able to take enforcement action against insider dealing, market manipulation, and other forms of market abuse involving cryptoassets. The FCA has identified crypto market abuse as a significant concern, noting the prevalence of pump-and-dump schemes, wash trading, and insider trading in the cryptoasset market. Until the market abuse regime is extended to cryptoassets, the FCA's enforcement tools are limited to the financial promotions regime and AML/CFT requirements, although general fraud offences under the Fraud Act 2006 may apply to certain forms of crypto market manipulation.

title: Ongoing Regulatory Reporting for Cryptoasset Firms
content: Cryptoasset firms registered under the MLR 2017 are subject to ongoing regulatory reporting requirements to the FCA. Firms must submit annual financial crime returns (REP-CRIM) reporting on the firm's compliance with AML/CFT obligations, including the number of SARs filed, the results of internal and external audits, and the firm's risk assessment. Firms must notify the FCA of certain material events without delay, including changes to the firm's business model or the types of cryptoasset services provided, changes to directors, beneficial owners, or other persons holding management functions, any significant operational incident including cybersecurity breaches, any legal proceedings commenced against the firm, any changes to the firm's AML/CFT framework, and any material outsourcing arrangement. Firms must also submit an annual MLR compliance return confirming ongoing compliance with the Regulations. The FCA charges annual fees to registered cryptoasset firms based on their revenue from cryptoasset activities. The fee structure was established through FCA CP21/16 and PS21/17, with minimum fees starting at approximately GBP 2,000 per year. Failure to submit required reports or pay fees is grounds for cancellation of MLR registration. The FCA has emphasised that registered firms are subject to ongoing supervisory engagement, not merely a one-time registration assessment.

title: Temporary Registration Regime for Existing Crypto Firms
content: When the MLR 2017 AML/CFT registration requirement for cryptoasset businesses came into force on 10 January 2020, the FCA established a Temporary Registration Regime (TRR) to allow existing businesses to continue operating while their applications were assessed. Firms that had been carrying on cryptoasset business before 10 January 2020 and had submitted a registration application before the deadline could continue operating under the TRR. The original TRR deadline was extended multiple times due to the volume and complexity of applications, ultimately running until 31 March 2022. During the TRR period, firms were required to comply with all AML/CFT obligations under the MLR 2017 despite not yet holding full registration. The FCA processed applications rigorously, and a significant proportion of firms either withdrew their applications or had them refused. By the end of the TRR, the FCA noted that many applicants did not meet the standards required for registration, with common deficiencies including inadequate customer due diligence procedures, insufficient transaction monitoring systems, lack of appropriately qualified Money Laundering Reporting Officers (MLROs), and deficient risk assessments. The FCA's experience with the TRR informed its approach to the broader crypto regulatory framework and underscored the need for firms to invest significantly in compliance infrastructure before seeking registration.

article: FCA GC23/1 — Guidance on Cryptoasset Financial Promotions
title: FCA Guidance on Compliance with Financial Promotion Rules
content: In September 2023, ahead of the October 2023 implementation date, the FCA published Guidance Consultation GC23/1 and subsequent finalised guidance on compliance with the new cryptoasset financial promotions regime. The guidance clarified several key operational requirements. For the Section 21 gateway (promotions approved by an authorised person), the FCA set out its expectations for the due diligence that authorised persons must conduct before approving a crypto firm's promotions, including assessment of the crypto firm's regulatory status, AML/CFT compliance, and the accuracy and fairness of the promotion content. The FCA emphasised that authorised firms that approve misleading crypto promotions may face enforcement action. For firms directly registered under the MLR, the guidance clarified that they may communicate their own promotions without approval by an authorised person, but must comply with all FCA promotion rules. The guidance also addressed social media promotions, stating that even short-form content on platforms such as X (Twitter), Instagram, and TikTok must include the required risk warnings. Influencer promotions must be clearly identifiable as financial promotions. The FCA confirmed it would take action against firms and individuals communicating non-compliant crypto promotions, including those based overseas targeting UK consumers.

article: UK National Risk Assessment of Money Laundering (2020)
title: UK Assessment of Crypto-Related Money Laundering Risk
content: HM Treasury's National Risk Assessment (NRA) of Money Laundering and Terrorist Financing, most recently updated in December 2020 with a further update in 2023, assessed the money laundering and terrorist financing risk associated with cryptoassets in the UK. The NRA classified the money laundering risk from cryptoassets as medium, noting that while the overall volume of illicit transactions through crypto remained a small proportion of total crypto transactions, the risk was increasing due to the growing adoption of cryptoassets, the increasing sophistication of money laundering techniques involving crypto, and the emergence of privacy coins and decentralised protocols designed to obscure transaction trails. The NRA identified key risk factors including the use of cryptoassets by organised crime groups for money laundering and drug proceeds, the use of cryptoassets for ransomware payments, the risks associated with non-compliant overseas exchanges accessible from the UK, and the challenges of monitoring and investigating DeFi transactions. The 2023 update elevated the terrorist financing risk associated with cryptoassets, noting increased use by terrorist organisations. The NRA informs the FCA's risk-based supervisory approach to cryptoasset firms and shapes the expectations set out in its Dear CEO letters and supervisory correspondence.

title: Digital Settlement Assets Framework Under FSMA 2023
content: Part 6 of the Financial Services and Markets Act 2023 introduces the concept of digital settlement assets (DSAs), defined as digital representations of value or rights that can be used for the settlement of payment obligations, are transferable and storable electronically using technology supporting the recording or storage of data, and that are not issued by a central bank. This definition is designed to capture fiat-backed stablecoins used for payments. FSMA 2023 provides powers to extend the Financial Market Infrastructure special administration regime to DSA service providers, ensuring that systemically important stablecoin payment systems can be wound down in an orderly manner if they fail. The Bank of England is empowered to oversee systemic DSA payment systems under Part 5 of the Banking Act 2009, as amended by FSMA 2023. HM Treasury may designate DSA payment systems as recognised payment systems, bringing them under the supervision of the Payment Systems Regulator (PSR). The FCA will be responsible for authorising and supervising DSA issuers and custodians under the regulatory framework to be established through secondary legislation. The DSA framework represents the UK's initial step in building a comprehensive regulatory regime for stablecoins that is distinct from and not directly transposing the EU's MiCA framework, reflecting the UK's post-Brexit approach to financial regulation.
